food_subgroup: Spiced toor dal and vegetable stew

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Sambar. Nearest parent is a cooked legume-vegetable stew. A new concept covering South Indian sambar preparations is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Legume-vegetable stew, South Indian style (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard side serving of sambar is 1 bowl (approximately 200g) — a moderately thin, richly spiced lentil and vegetable stew poured alongside idli, dosa, or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Sambar — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN does not have a sambar composite dish entry. Values are derived from ICMR-NIN toor dal composition data combined with standard sambar recipe proportions (toor dal ~15%, mixed vegetables ~20%, tamarind ~3%, spices/oil ~5%, water balance). Cross-referenced with available published sambar nutrition data.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from toor dal (pigeon pea) GI; legume-based stew with significant fibre content gives a low GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 640mg per bowl (200g) — elevated; restaurant sambar is notoriously high in salt; home-cooked sambar can be prepared with half the salt.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt sambar or limit their sambar serving to 100–150g. Sambar made at home allows precise sodium control — target under 300mg sodium per bowl.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~35) due to toor dal and vegetable fibre; glycaemic load per bowl is approximately 6 — negligible.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambar is highly beneficial for diabetic patients — pairing sambar with idli, dosa, or rice significantly lowers the overall glycaemic response of the meal. Encourage liberal sambar consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Turmeric and tamarind have potent anti-inflammatory properties; drumstick (moringa) is exceptionally rich in iron and vitamin C; toor dal provides folate.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sambar is highly beneficial for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. The turmeric content (curcumin) and drumstick (moringa) make it one of the most anti-inflammatory foods in South Indian cuisine.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambar is the quintessential South Indian lentil and vegetable stew — a moderately thin, deeply flavoured, tangy-spicy broth made from pressure-cooked toor dal (split pigeon peas) blended with tamarind water and a robust sambar masala spice blend. It typically contains drumstick pods, brinjal, tomato, and onion, finished with a mustard seed and curry leaf tadka. Sambar is served as an essential accompaniment to idli, dosa, vada, and rice across South India and Singapore's Indian restaurants. It is vegan, gluten-free, and an excellent source of plant protein and fibre.

confidence_reason: No standalone sambar entry in ICMR-NIN or HPB Foodo. Values estimated from component ingredients per ICMR-NIN data and standard recipe proportions. Sodium value reflects typical restaurant preparation and may vary significantly.
notes: Vegan and gluten-free. Sodium is the primary health concern — restaurant sambar can contain 300–600mg sodium per 100g depending on preparation. Drumstick (moringa) pods, if included, significantly increase iron and vitamin C content. Sambar recipe varies widely by family and region.
